description: 35 scenarios per CEFR level: 18 work + 17 life. Each firstLine: brief scene + "I'll start as the …; you reply as the …." — then the in-character opening. Server picks randomly by level.

scenarios.title: Office — sign in
scenarios.setting: You visit an office. You must sign in at the desk.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Office — sign in. You visit an office. You must sign in at the desk. I'll start as the receptionist; you reply as the visitor. — Hello. What is your name? And who are you here to see today?
learnerGoals: Say one person or team name.

scenarios.title: Front desk check-in
scenarios.setting: You arrive for a meeting and the reception desk needs basic details before they print a badge.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Front desk check-in. You arrive for a meeting and the reception desk needs basic details before they print a badge. I'll start as the receptionist; you reply as the visitor. — Hi there — can I get your full name, the company you are with, and who you are meeting?

scenarios.title: Visitor registration
scenarios.setting: You are checking in at a company lobby; security rules require a host name and ID.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Visitor registration. You are checking in at a company lobby; security rules require a host name and ID. I'll start as the receptionist; you reply as the visitor. — Welcome. I need your ID for the visitor log, the name of your host, and whether you have a laptop to register — what should I put down?

scenarios.title: Lobby registration under tightened policy
scenarios.setting: New lobby rules require purpose of visit and equipment; you want a smooth check-in without delays.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Lobby registration under tightened policy. New lobby rules require purpose of visit and equipment; you want a smooth check-in without delays. I'll start as the receptionist; you reply as the visitor. — We have to capture visit purpose and any devices on the badge request — walk me through who invited you, what you are delivering or discussing, and what you are carrying in.

scenarios.title: High-security visitor intake
scenarios.setting: You are visiting a site with split compliance rules; the receptionist must reconcile access and confidentiality.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: High-security visitor intake. You are visiting a site with split compliance rules; the receptionist must reconcile access and confidentiality. I'll start as the receptionist; you reply as the visitor. — Before I issue access, I need to align three things: who sponsored you, whether your discussion touches confidential data, and what level of floor access your host pre-approved — how do you want to frame that?

scenarios.title: Executive site visit — access boundaries
scenarios.setting: A sensitive briefing overlaps with a wider team floor; you must negotiate what access is defensible.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Executive site visit — access boundaries. A sensitive briefing overlaps with a wider team floor; you must negotiate what access is defensible. I'll start as the receptionist; you reply as the visitor. — We are tight on scope today: I can either route you to the executive briefing room under escort, or widen badge access with legal sign-off — which risk trade-off does your host want to own, and what are you explicitly not cleared to hear?

scenarios.title: Shop — shift change
scenarios.setting: Your work shift is ending. Your supervisor asks a short question.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Shop — shift change. Your work shift is ending. Your supervisor asks a short question. I'll start as the shift supervisor; you reply as the sales assistant. — Hi — quick handover. What is low in stock, and did any customer complain today?

scenarios.title: Retail handover
scenarios.setting: You finish a shift at a small shop and must pass notes to the next staff.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Retail handover. You finish a shift at a small shop and must pass notes to the next staff. I'll start as the shift supervisor; you reply as the sales assistant. — Before you clock out — what ran low on the floor, any returns I should know about, and anything odd with the card reader?

scenarios.title: End-of-shift retail notes
scenarios.setting: You hand over to the closing supervisor; they need inventory and customer issues.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: End-of-shift retail notes. You hand over to the closing supervisor; they need inventory and customer issues. I'll start as the shift supervisor; you reply as the sales assistant. — I need a tight handover: top three SKUs that moved, any unhappy customers and how you handled it, and whether cash matches the drawer — what is your summary?

scenarios.title: Shift turnover with shrink risk
scenarios.setting: Inventory discrepancies rose last week; handover must flag patterns without blame.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Shift turnover with shrink risk. Inventory discrepancies rose last week; handover must flag patterns without blame. I'll start as the shift supervisor; you reply as the sales assistant. — Give me the operational picture: what sold through, what felt off in counts or voids, and what you want the next shift to double-check before close.

scenarios.title: Handover under audit pressure
scenarios.setting: Auditors asked for traceable notes; you must balance speed and accountability.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Handover under audit pressure. Auditors asked for traceable notes; you must balance speed and accountability. I'll start as the shift supervisor; you reply as the sales assistant. — I need a defensible handover — what changed materially since open, what evidence supports it, and what you are uncertain about that I should escalate rather than assume?

scenarios.title: Crisis week retail continuity
scenarios.setting: Supply noise and staff shortages overlap; the handover must set strategic priorities.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Crisis week retail continuity. Supply noise and staff shortages overlap; the handover must set strategic priorities. I'll start as the shift supervisor; you reply as the sales assistant. — Frame this not as tasks but risk: what threatens revenue or compliance tonight, what you mitigated, and what decision you need from me in the next ten minutes — with one hard number if possible.

scenarios.title: Quick work question
scenarios.setting: Your boss asks what you did today at work.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Quick work question. Your boss asks what you did today at work. I'll start as the team lead; you reply as the team member. — Hi. What did you finish today? And what is your problem — one thing only?

scenarios.title: Short stand-up
scenarios.setting: Your team has a quick daily meeting; you give a short update.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Short stand-up. Your team has a quick daily meeting; you give a short update. I'll start as the team lead; you reply as the team member. — Quick round — what did you complete since yesterday, and what is blocking you right now?

scenarios.title: Daily standup update
scenarios.setting: You report progress and one blocker in a time-boxed standup.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Daily standup update. You report progress and one blocker in a time-boxed standup. I'll start as the team lead; you reply as the team member. — We only have a minute each — what shipped, what is stuck, and what help do you need from the room?

scenarios.title: Standup with cross-team dependency
scenarios.setting: Your update must surface a dependency without derailing the whole meeting.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Standup with cross-team dependency. Your update must surface a dependency without derailing the whole meeting. I'll start as the team lead; you reply as the team member. — Give your update in three beats: outcome, confidence level, and the one dependency that could slip the date if we ignore it — what are they?

scenarios.title: Standup under delivery pressure
scenarios.setting: Leadership is listening for honesty versus optimism; you calibrate the message.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Standup under delivery pressure. Leadership is listening for honesty versus optimism; you calibrate the message. I'll start as the team lead; you reply as the team member. — I want signal, not morale — what is objectively done, what is fragile, and what would you stop doing this week to protect the commitment we already made?

scenarios.title: Executive stand-in standup
scenarios.setting: You represent a team in front of a skeptical director.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Executive stand-in standup. You represent a team in front of a skeptical director. I'll start as the team lead; you reply as the team member. — In one minute: defend the current milestone, name the single assumption that could invalidate our plan, and tell me what authority you need unlocked — no filler.

scenarios.title: Email problem
scenarios.setting: A coworker thinks you did not answer an email.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Email problem. A coworker thinks you did not answer an email. I'll start as the coworker; you reply as the project teammate. — Hi. I sent you an email yesterday. Did you see it? Can you answer today?

scenarios.title: Missed email follow-up
scenarios.setting: A colleague needs a reply you may have missed.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Missed email follow-up. A colleague needs a reply you may have missed. I'll start as the coworker; you reply as the project teammate. — Hey — I emailed yesterday about the shared doc. Did it reach you, and when can you take a look?

scenarios.title: Chasing a project email
scenarios.setting: A deadline depends on your input; your coworker checks in politely but firmly.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Chasing a project email. A deadline depends on your input; your coworker checks in politely but firmly. I'll start as the coworker; you reply as the project teammate. — I need your sign-off on the draft by tomorrow — can you confirm you saw my thread, and if not, what is the fastest way to get your eyes on it today?

scenarios.title: Thread drift and ownership
scenarios.setting: Multiple emails crossed; you clarify ownership without damaging the relationship.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Thread drift and ownership. Multiple emails crossed; you clarify ownership without damaging the relationship. I'll start as the coworker; you reply as the project teammate. — We have parallel threads and conflicting versions — can you tell me which file you treat as source of truth, and whether you want me to consolidate or you will?

scenarios.title: Escalation risk on communication gap
scenarios.setting: Stakeholders inferred silence as delay; you repair process and substance.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Escalation risk on communication gap. Stakeholders inferred silence as delay; you repair process and substance. I'll start as the coworker; you reply as the project teammate. — Silence got interpreted as a slip — walk me through what you actually blocked on, what you need from me to unblock, and how we prevent the next miss without more process theater.

scenarios.title: Political cost of inbox debt
scenarios.setting: A partner team is building a narrative; you reset norms at senior peer level.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Political cost of inbox debt. A partner team is building a narrative; you reset norms at senior peer level. I'll start as the coworker; you reply as the project teammate. — I am going to be direct: your non-response became ammunition in a prioritization fight — what is the accurate story, what trade-off you were protecting, and what explicit SLA do you want going forward?

scenarios.title: Client phone — order
scenarios.setting: A client calls about an order. You answer.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Client phone — order. A client calls about an order. You answer. I'll start as the client services; you reply as the account manager. — Hello, this is support. What is your order number? What is the problem?

scenarios.title: Customer order call
scenarios.setting: A customer calls about a wrong shipment; you gather facts.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Customer order call. A customer calls about a wrong shipment; you gather facts. I'll start as the client services; you reply as the account manager. — Thanks for calling — can you give me the order ID, what you expected, and what arrived instead?

scenarios.title: Shipment mismatch triage
scenarios.setting: You represent the vendor; the client is annoyed but reasonable.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Shipment mismatch triage. You represent the vendor; the client is annoyed but reasonable. I'll start as the client services; you reply as the account manager. — I want to fix this quickly — confirm the PO, the SKU you expected versus what the packing slip shows, and whether you can send a photo so we can authorize a replacement.

scenarios.title: Commercial relationship under error
scenarios.setting: The error touches a renewal; you balance remedy and contract language.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Commercial relationship under error. The error touches a renewal; you balance remedy and contract language. I'll start as the client services; you reply as the account manager. — Walk me through timeline impact, what the client already told their boss, and whether we are discussing a credit, a redo, or a process fix — I need one recommendation.

scenarios.title: Key account recovery call
scenarios.setting: Trust is dented; you probe root cause and signal seriousness.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Key account recovery call. Trust is dented; you probe root cause and signal seriousness. I'll start as the client services; you reply as the account manager. — Before we talk solution, I want the narrative straight: what broke in our chain, what you already promised internally, and what would count as a credible repair beyond a one-off exception?

scenarios.title: Contractual exposure on repeated failures
scenarios.setting: Legal and ops are in the loop; you negotiate a bounded settlement.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Contractual exposure on repeated failures. Legal and ops are in the loop; you negotiate a bounded settlement. I'll start as the client services; you reply as the account manager. — We are past goodwill gestures — map the failure mode, quantify business impact conservatively, and tell me what remedy stays inside our standard terms versus what needs an amendment.

scenarios.title: Computer password
scenarios.setting: You cannot log in. IT helps you.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Computer password. You cannot log in. IT helps you. I'll start as the iT help desk; you reply as the employee. — Hi, IT here. What is your work email? Can you tell me the error message on the screen?

scenarios.title: Password reset request
scenarios.setting: You locked your account; IT verifies identity and resets access.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Password reset request. You locked your account; IT verifies identity and resets access. I'll start as the iT help desk; you reply as the employee. — I can help — what is your employee ID or email, and are you on a work laptop or personal device right now?

scenarios.title: Access lockout triage
scenarios.setting: You need tools for a deadline; IT follows security steps.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Access lockout triage. You need tools for a deadline; IT follows security steps. I'll start as the iT help desk; you reply as the employee. — Let us get you back in — confirm your username, whether MFA is prompting, and if you recently changed devices so we pick the right reset path.

scenarios.title: Security exception under deadline
scenarios.setting: Policy blocks a fast reset; you negotiate a compliant workaround.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Security exception under deadline. Policy blocks a fast reset; you negotiate a compliant workaround. I'll start as the iT help desk; you reply as the employee. — Standard reset will cost you hours — tell me the business deadline, what systems you must reach, and whether your manager can approve a temporary elevated path.

scenarios.title: Insider risk vs productivity
scenarios.setting: Unusual login patterns triggered controls; you explain trade-offs.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Insider risk vs productivity. Unusual login patterns triggered controls; you explain trade-offs. I'll start as the iT help desk; you reply as the employee. — We flagged activity that looks like credential sharing — help me understand legitimate context, what access you actually need, and what audit trail we can leave that satisfies security without blocking delivery.

scenarios.title: Post-breach tightening
scenarios.setting: Org-wide controls landed mid-project; you align exception governance.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Post-breach tightening. Org-wide controls landed mid-project; you align exception governance. I'll start as the iT help desk; you reply as the employee. — We are not debating convenience — which workflows are safety-critical, which assumptions in your project require exceptions, and who will sign the residual risk if we grant them?

scenarios.title: Interview time
scenarios.setting: You plan an interview with a recruiter.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Interview time. You plan an interview with a recruiter. I'll start as the recruiter; you reply as the hiring manager. — Hi. When can you interview the candidate? Morning or afternoon?

scenarios.title: Scheduling a candidate
scenarios.setting: You coordinate a simple interview slot with recruiting.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Scheduling a candidate. You coordinate a simple interview slot with recruiting. I'll start as the recruiter; you reply as the hiring manager. — We have a strong candidate — what days work for your panel, and do you prefer video or on-site?

scenarios.title: Panel interview logistics
scenarios.setting: Multiple calendars conflict; you lock a workable slot.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Panel interview logistics. Multiple calendars conflict; you lock a workable slot. I'll start as the recruiter; you reply as the hiring manager. — I need a 90-minute panel next week — who must be in the room, what flexibility do you have on day, and should we record a debrief right after?

scenarios.title: Competing priorities on hiring
scenarios.setting: Delivery pressure fights interview time; you negotiate a fair slot.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Competing priorities on hiring. Delivery pressure fights interview time; you negotiate a fair slot. I'll start as the recruiter; you reply as the hiring manager. — Your team is underwater — tell me the minimum viable panel, the latest acceptable date without losing the candidate, and what you want assessed versus deferred to a second round.

scenarios.title: Calibration before final round
scenarios.setting: Bias and bar drift are concerns; you align evaluation criteria.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Calibration before final round. Bias and bar drift are concerns; you align evaluation criteria. I'll start as the recruiter; you reply as the hiring manager. — Before we schedule, I want the rubric explicit — what differentiates hire from no-hire for you, what evidence you need in-round, and how we document decisions if the panel splits?

scenarios.title: Executive search timing conflict
scenarios.setting: Board timeline compresses hiring; you manage optics and process.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Executive search timing conflict. Board timeline compresses hiring; you manage optics and process. I'll start as the recruiter; you reply as the hiring manager. — We are being pushed to shortcut diligence — what are we willing to stake on a fast decision, what must not be skipped without reputational risk, and who owns the narrative if this hire misfires?

scenarios.title: Expense question
scenarios.setting: Finance asks about your expense report.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Expense question. Finance asks about your expense report. I'll start as the finance analyst; you reply as the employee. — Hello. One line on your report is not clear. What is this $40 for?

scenarios.title: Expense line item check
scenarios.setting: A receipt is missing or unclear; finance emails you.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Expense line item check. A receipt is missing or unclear; finance emails you. I'll start as the finance analyst; you reply as the employee. — Can you clarify the dinner charge on May 3 — who attended and was it client-facing?

scenarios.title: Expense policy clarification
scenarios.setting: A claim sits in limbo; you resolve it with finance.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Expense policy clarification. A claim sits in limbo; you resolve it with finance. I'll start as the finance analyst; you reply as the employee. — We need documentation for the mileage block — what route, business purpose, and can you attach a map or meeting invite so we can release payment?

scenarios.title: Gray-area travel spend
scenarios.setting: Policy interpretation differs by region; you seek a consistent ruling.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Gray-area travel spend. Policy interpretation differs by region; you seek a consistent ruling. I'll start as the finance analyst; you reply as the employee. — This looks like mixed personal and business travel — walk me through intent, what was incremental cost, and what your manager already approved verbally so we can align with policy.

scenarios.title: Audit sampling hits your claims
scenarios.setting: Sampling expanded; you defend legitimacy without being defensive.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Audit sampling hits your claims. Sampling expanded; you defend legitimacy without being defensive. I'll start as the finance analyst; you reply as the employee. — Auditors want narrative, not receipts alone — what business outcome each disputed spend supported, who benefited, and what control gap we should close so this does not repeat?

scenarios.title: Fraud signal investigation
scenarios.setting: Pattern triggers review; you cooperate at principle level.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Fraud signal investigation. Pattern triggers review; you cooperate at principle level. I'll start as the finance analyst; you reply as the employee. — We are obligated to ask hard questions — help me reconstruct timelines, identify anyone with shared card access, and tell me what innocent explanation fits the data without asking you to guess our thresholds.

scenarios.title: Meeting room
scenarios.setting: Two people want the same room.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Meeting room. Two people want the same room. I'll start as the office coordinator; you reply as the team member. — Hi. Room B is booked for two meetings. Which meeting can move?

scenarios.title: Double-booked room
scenarios.setting: You mediate a scheduling clash for a meeting room.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Double-booked room. You mediate a scheduling clash for a meeting room. I'll start as the office coordinator; you reply as the team member. — Both teams booked Room B at 2 — which meeting is easier to move, and do you need AV for the alternative?

scenarios.title: Room conflict resolution
scenarios.setting: A client workshop conflicts with an internal sync.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Room conflict resolution. A client workshop conflicts with an internal sync. I'll start as the office coordinator; you reply as the team member. — We have a hard conflict on the large room — which event has external stakeholders, what is the backup space you can accept, and how much setup time do you need?

scenarios.title: Resource contention week
scenarios.setting: Multiple teams claim priority; you need a decision rule.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Resource contention week. Multiple teams claim priority; you need a decision rule. I'll start as the office coordinator; you reply as the team member. — Give me the business impact of each meeting, whether either can go hybrid, and what executive sponsor should break the tie if neither moves voluntarily.

scenarios.title: Facilities under cost review
scenarios.setting: Room consolidation is rumored; you negotiate workable norms.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Facilities under cost review. Room consolidation is rumored; you negotiate workable norms. I'll start as the office coordinator; you reply as the team member. — We are being asked to cut footprint — which meetings truly require physical presence, what hybrid standard you can live with, and what fairness principle should govern recurring holds versus ad hoc?

scenarios.title: HQ redesign politics
scenarios.setting: Leadership uses space as power signal; you argue for operational reality.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: HQ redesign politics. Leadership uses space as power signal; you argue for operational reality. I'll start as the office coordinator; you reply as the team member. — Space decisions are becoming a proxy for status — make the case for what work actually requires colocation, what signal sends to clients, and what political cost you are willing to absorb to protect deep-work capacity.

scenarios.title: Boss asks progress
scenarios.setting: Your manager asks a simple question in the hall.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Boss asks progress. Your manager asks a simple question in the hall. I'll start as the manager; you reply as the individual contributor. — Hi. How is the report? Is it finished today?

scenarios.title: Quick status check
scenarios.setting: Your manager wants a brief update on a task.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Quick status check. Your manager wants a brief update on a task. I'll start as the manager; you reply as the individual contributor. — Hey — where are we on the client deck, and do you need anything from me to finish?

scenarios.title: Manager checkpoint
scenarios.setting: You owe a concise status on a slipping task.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Manager checkpoint. You owe a concise status on a slipping task. I'll start as the manager; you reply as the individual contributor. — I need a honest snapshot: percent complete, what changed since Monday, and whether you are still comfortable with Friday — what is your read?

scenarios.title: Pressure without micromanagement
scenarios.setting: Your boss senses risk; you re-establish trust with specifics.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Pressure without micromanagement. Your boss senses risk; you re-establish trust with specifics. I'll start as the manager; you reply as the individual contributor. — Help me understand variance — what assumptions broke, what you already mitigated, and what decision you need from me today versus what you can own silently?

scenarios.title: Credibility repair after a miss
scenarios.setting: A prior deadline slipped; you frame learning and controls.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Credibility repair after a miss. A prior deadline slipped; you frame learning and controls. I'll start as the manager; you reply as the individual contributor. — I am not looking for excuses — what systemic issue caused the miss, what you changed this week, and what early warning metric you want me to watch next time?

scenarios.title: Career-limiting visibility
scenarios.setting: Executives linked your name to a failure narrative.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Career-limiting visibility. Executives linked your name to a failure narrative. I'll start as the manager; you reply as the individual contributor. — We need a narrative reset grounded in fact — what you owned versus what was ambiguous in scope, what evidence supports your version, and what risky project you still want after this?

scenarios.title: Late delivery call
scenarios.setting: Your order is late. You call the company.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Late delivery call. Your order is late. You call the company. I'll start as the vendor account rep; you reply as the buyer. — Hello. My order is late. When will it arrive?

scenarios.title: Supplier delay
scenarios.setting: Materials are late; you ask for a realistic ETA.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Supplier delay. Materials are late; you ask for a realistic ETA. I'll start as the vendor account rep; you reply as the buyer. — Our PO 4477 is past due — what is the revised ship date and what caused the delay?

scenarios.title: Vendor slip impacts production
scenarios.setting: You need a recovery plan, not apologies.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Vendor slip impacts production. You need a recovery plan, not apologies. I'll start as the vendor account rep; you reply as the buyer. — This hits our line Friday — confirm root cause, give me a date you will stake your name on, and tell me what expedite options exist at what cost.

scenarios.title: Contractual remedy discussion
scenarios.setting: SLA language is fuzzy; you probe remedies without lawyering every line.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Contractual remedy discussion. SLA language is fuzzy; you probe remedies without lawyering every line. I'll start as the vendor account rep; you reply as the buyer. — Walk me through force majeure versus operational failure, what credits you are authorized to offer, and how we document this so procurement does not fight finance.

scenarios.title: Strategic supplier relationship
scenarios.setting: You cannot burn the bridge; you still need leverage.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Strategic supplier relationship. You cannot burn the bridge; you still need leverage. I'll start as the vendor account rep; you reply as the buyer. — We are evaluating dual-sourcing — what structural changes you will commit to prevent recurrence, what transparency you will give on capacity, and what economics make staying preferred for both sides?

scenarios.title: Board-level supply shock
scenarios.setting: Disclosure timing matters; you negotiate transparency and liability.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Board-level supply shock. Disclosure timing matters; you negotiate transparency and liability. I'll start as the vendor account rep; you reply as the buyer. — Material impact is likely — what do you know versus suspect, what are you willing to put in writing today, and what governance do we need if this crosses into customer-facing commitments?

scenarios.title: Too loud at work
scenarios.setting: Your neighbor at work is loud. You speak politely.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Too loud at work. Your neighbor at work is loud. You speak politely. I'll start as the deskmate; you reply as the colleague. — Excuse me. Please talk a little quieter. I need quiet to work.

scenarios.title: Noise in open office
scenarios.setting: You ask a coworker to lower call volume.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Noise in open office. You ask a coworker to lower call volume. I'll start as the deskmate; you reply as the colleague. — Hey — I am on a tight deadline; could you take long calls in the booth or lower your voice a bit?

scenarios.title: Open floor distraction
scenarios.setting: Repeated loud meetings at a nearby desk hurt focus.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Open floor distraction. Repeated loud meetings at a nearby desk hurt focus. I'll start as the deskmate; you reply as the colleague. — I do not want to be difficult — your calls are carrying. Can we agree on booth use for longer conversations, or different hours for speakerphone?

scenarios.title: Norms conflict on hybrid floor
scenarios.setting: Team culture clashes with concentration needs.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Norms conflict on hybrid floor. Team culture clashes with concentration needs. I'll start as the deskmate; you reply as the colleague. — We are misaligned on what open plan means — can we define quiet hours or a signal for deep work, and will you help enforce it peer to peer?

scenarios.title: Psychological safety vs productivity
scenarios.setting: A popular teammate is the source of noise; politics matter.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Psychological safety vs productivity. A popular teammate is the source of noise; politics matter. I'll start as the deskmate; you reply as the colleague. — I need this framed as shared norms, not personal criticism — how do we protect collaboration energy without normalizing constant disruption, and what experiment can we try for two weeks?

scenarios.title: Executive floor etiquette dispute
scenarios.setting: Visibility escalates minor friction; you reset leadership example.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Executive floor etiquette dispute. Visibility escalates minor friction; you reset leadership example. I'll start as the deskmate; you reply as the colleague. — This became symbolic — what behavior signals we want from leaders in open space, what exceptions are defensible, and how do we avoid a policy memo that kills spontaneity?

scenarios.title: Review help
scenarios.setting: HR helps you prepare for a work review.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Review help. HR helps you prepare for a work review. I'll start as the hR partner; you reply as the employee. — Hi. Your review is next week. What work do you want to talk about?

scenarios.title: Performance review prep
scenarios.setting: You discuss what evidence to bring to a review.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Performance review prep. You discuss what evidence to bring to a review. I'll start as the hR partner; you reply as the employee. — Before your review — what wins do you want highlighted, and where do you want growth feedback?

scenarios.title: Review narrative alignment
scenarios.setting: You align on goals and evidence with HR before the manager conversation.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Review narrative alignment. You align on goals and evidence with HR before the manager conversation. I'll start as the hR partner; you reply as the employee. — Help me pressure-test your story — what outcomes you owned versus supported, metrics you can cite, and concerns you want aired constructively?

scenarios.title: Calibration awareness
scenarios.setting: Ratings are competitive; you position without sounding defensive.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Calibration awareness. Ratings are competitive; you position without sounding defensive. I'll start as the hR partner; you reply as the employee. — What differentiates your impact from peers at your level, what stretch you took on, and where do you want the conversation to land on development versus compensation?

scenarios.title: Bias-aware review coaching
scenarios.setting: Identity dynamics may affect perception; you prepare language.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Bias-aware review coaching. Identity dynamics may affect perception; you prepare language. I'll start as the hR partner; you reply as the employee. — Where might your contributions be under-visible, what third-party validation helps, and how do you want to redirect if feedback drifts into style over substance?

scenarios.title: Executive promotion discussion
scenarios.setting: Stakeholders disagree on readiness; you craft a principled case.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Executive promotion discussion. Stakeholders disagree on readiness; you craft a principled case. I'll start as the hR partner; you reply as the employee. — What bar you are being held to implicitly versus explicitly, what risks sponsors are weighing, and what trade-off you propose if promotion is deferred — with timeline?

scenarios.title: First day call
scenarios.setting: It is your first day at a new job from home.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: First day call. It is your first day at a new job from home. I'll start as the onboarding buddy; you reply as the new hire. — Welcome! What tools can you open? Do you see your email?

scenarios.title: Remote onboarding check
scenarios.setting: A buddy helps you access systems on day one.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Remote onboarding check. A buddy helps you access systems on day one. I'll start as the onboarding buddy; you reply as the new hire. — Let us verify access — can you log into chat, VPN, and the HR portal, and tell me where you are stuck?

scenarios.title: Day-one remote setup
scenarios.setting: You need a prioritized list to become productive quickly.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Day-one remote setup. You need a prioritized list to become productive quickly. I'll start as the onboarding buddy; you reply as the new hire. — I want a realistic first-week plan — what accounts matter most, who owns approvals, and what meetings you should not skip?

scenarios.title: Onboarding friction across time zones
scenarios.setting: Delays cascade; you negotiate a workable ramp.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Onboarding friction across time zones. Delays cascade; you negotiate a workable ramp. I'll start as the onboarding buddy; you reply as the new hire. — What blockers are process versus tooling, what can async, and what synchronous touchpoints you need from your manager so you are not idle three days?

scenarios.title: Cultural assimilation remote
scenarios.setting: Informal knowledge transfer is weak; you address it explicitly.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Cultural assimilation remote. Informal knowledge transfer is weak; you address it explicitly. I'll start as the onboarding buddy; you reply as the new hire. — What unwritten rules I should know, who actually decides things versus who appears to, and what network gaps put me at a disadvantage if we do not fix them early?

scenarios.title: Merger onboarding ambiguity
scenarios.setting: Dual systems and loyalty questions; you navigate identity.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Merger onboarding ambiguity. Dual systems and loyalty questions; you navigate identity. I'll start as the onboarding buddy; you reply as the new hire. — Which norms survive the integration, where are we pretending alignment while competing internally, and what do you need from me to model constructive ambiguity?

scenarios.title: Too much work
scenarios.setting: Someone wants work very fast. You say it is hard.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Too much work. Someone wants work very fast. You say it is hard. I'll start as the product manager; you reply as the engineer. — Hi. Friday is very fast for me. Can we change the date?

scenarios.title: Tight deadline
scenarios.setting: You push back politely on an aggressive date.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Tight deadline. You push back politely on an aggressive date. I'll start as the product manager; you reply as the engineer. — Friday is risky for quality — what can we cut from scope or move to a follow-up release?

scenarios.title: Scope versus date trade-off
scenarios.setting: You negotiate what ships on the original milestone.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Scope versus date trade-off. You negotiate what ships on the original milestone. I'll start as the product manager; you reply as the engineer. — I can hit Friday if we freeze requirements today and drop the analytics slice — which cut hurts the business less, and who approves it?

scenarios.title: Commitment engineering under sales pressure
scenarios.setting: Commercial promises pre-empted technical reality.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Commitment engineering under sales pressure. Commercial promises pre-empted technical reality. I'll start as the product manager; you reply as the engineer. — Help me map what was promised externally versus what is feasible — what narrative we give sales, what buffer we hide responsibly, and what governance stops this recurring?

scenarios.title: Organizational overcommitment
scenarios.setting: You challenge systemic overload, not just this ticket.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Organizational overcommitment. You challenge systemic overload, not just this ticket. I'll start as the product manager; you reply as the engineer. — We are burning credibility — what portfolio decision you will defend to leadership, what you will stop starting, and what metric proves we are lying to ourselves about capacity?

scenarios.title: Pre-mortem before a flagship launch
scenarios.setting: Careers ride on the date; you force explicit risk ownership.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Pre-mortem before a flagship launch. Careers ride on the date; you force explicit risk ownership. I'll start as the product manager; you reply as the engineer. — If this launches and fails, whose decision tree gets scrutinized — what are we shipping with known defects, who signs residual risk, and what kill criteria we will actually honor?

scenarios.title: Waiting on another team
scenarios.setting: Your work waits for another team. You ask them.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Waiting on another team. Your work waits for another team. You ask them. I'll start as the partner team lead; you reply as the project owner. — Hello. We need your API. When can you finish it?

scenarios.title: Dependency delay
scenarios.setting: Another team blocks your milestone; you request a date.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Dependency delay. Another team blocks your milestone; you request a date. I'll start as the partner team lead; you reply as the project owner. — We are blocked on your schema change — what is realistic for delivery, and can we get a daily checkpoint until it lands?

scenarios.title: Unblocking a shared dependency
scenarios.setting: Two teams share accountability; you clarify handoffs.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Unblocking a shared dependency. Two teams share accountability; you clarify handoffs. I'll start as the partner team lead; you reply as the project owner. — I need a joint plan: your remaining tasks, our integration tests, and a single owner for cutover — what do you propose?

scenarios.title: Political dependency gridlock
scenarios.setting: Priorities conflict; you seek escalation path.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Political dependency gridlock. Priorities conflict; you seek escalation path. I'll start as the partner team lead; you reply as the project owner. — We are misaligned on priority — what your team owes versus what mine does, where the dispute should be decided, and what interim workaround protects users?

scenarios.title: Matrix organization stalemate
scenarios.setting: Incentives misalign; you reframe shared OKRs.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Matrix organization stalemate. Incentives misalign; you reframe shared OKRs. I'll start as the partner team lead; you reply as the project owner. — What outcome we both get graded on, what local optimization is hurting it, and what executive alignment you need me to help secure without making you look bad?

scenarios.title: Ecosystem partnership tension
scenarios.setting: External partner and internal team blame each other.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Ecosystem partnership tension. External partner and internal team blame each other. I'll start as the partner team lead; you reply as the project owner. — Separate narrative from obligation — what contract and what handshake actually govern behavior, what evidence each side has, and what joint steering body can decide without litigation vibes?

scenarios.title: Angry customer
scenarios.setting: A customer is unhappy on the phone.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Angry customer. A customer is unhappy on the phone. I'll start as the angry customer; you reply as the support agent. — I am upset! My product is broken! What will you do?

scenarios.title: Handling a complaint
scenarios.setting: A customer raises voice about a defect; you stay calm.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Handling a complaint. A customer raises voice about a defect; you stay calm. I'll start as the angry customer; you reply as the support agent. — I hear you — let us fix this. What is the product, when did it fail, and what do you want as a fair outcome?

scenarios.title: Escalated support call
scenarios.setting: You de-escalate while gathering facts for a refund decision.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Escalated support call. You de-escalate while gathering facts for a refund decision. I'll start as the angry customer; you reply as the support agent. — I am sorry this happened — walk me through what you tried, what broke our promise, and whether replacement or refund gets you whole faster.

scenarios.title: Retention-risk complaint
scenarios.setting: Social media threat is implied; you balance policy and brand.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Retention-risk complaint. Social media threat is implied; you balance policy and brand. I'll start as the angry customer; you reply as the support agent. — Help me understand public-facing risk, what you already posted, what remedy closes the loop privately, and what timeline you consider reasonable?

scenarios.title: Values clash with policy
scenarios.setting: Customer cites fairness; policy is rigid; you interpret humanely.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Values clash with policy. Customer cites fairness; policy is rigid; you interpret humanely. I'll start as the angry customer; you reply as the support agent. — Where is policy failing your legitimate expectation, what precedent worries you if we say no, and what creative within-bounds fix signals we listened?

scenarios.title: Regulatory undertone complaint
scenarios.setting: Legal language appears; you avoid admissions while repairing.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Regulatory undertone complaint. Legal language appears; you avoid admissions while repairing. I'll start as the angry customer; you reply as the support agent. — What counsel advised you, what facts are undisputed, what remedy you seek that is not punitive, and how do we document resolution without creating liability theater?

scenarios.title: Training room
scenarios.setting: A trainer checks if you can hear before a class.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Training room. A trainer checks if you can hear before a class. I'll start as the trainer; you reply as the participant. — Hello. Can you hear me? Is your microphone on?

scenarios.title: Workshop tech check
scenarios.setting: You join a training session; the host tests audio.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Workshop tech check. You join a training session; the host tests audio. I'll start as the trainer; you reply as the participant. — Quick AV check — can you hear me clearly, and can you unmute and say your name?

scenarios.title: Hybrid training readiness
scenarios.setting: Some people are remote; you confirm participation modes.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Hybrid training readiness. Some people are remote; you confirm participation modes. I'll start as the trainer; you reply as the participant. — We are hybrid today — confirm your camera policy, whether slides are visible, and if breakout rooms will work on your device.

scenarios.title: Facilitator under tool failure
scenarios.setting: Platform glitches; you co-create a fallback.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Facilitator under tool failure. Platform glitches; you co-create a fallback. I'll start as the trainer; you reply as the participant. — We lost screen share — what alternative channel works for you, how we preserve engagement, and whether we should pause versus push audio-only?

scenarios.title: Global audience accessibility
scenarios.setting: Captions, language, and bandwidth vary; you negotiate norms.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Global audience accessibility. Captions, language, and bandwidth vary; you negotiate norms. I'll start as the trainer; you reply as the participant. — What accessibility needs we have not surfaced, what pace disadvantages non-native speakers, and what async supplement makes this fair without doubling your prep?

scenarios.title: Executive academy credibility
scenarios.setting: VIP participants challenge format; you defend design.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Executive academy credibility. VIP participants challenge format; you defend design. I'll start as the trainer; you reply as the participant. — What skepticism about this format is valid, what learning science we are betting on, and what concession you will make without turning this into a passive webinar?

scenarios.title: Pharmacy — medicine
scenarios.setting: You need simple help at a pharmacy.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Pharmacy — medicine. You need simple help at a pharmacy. I'll start as the pharmacist; you reply as the customer. — Hello. I need medicine for a headache. Can you help me?

scenarios.title: Over-the-counter advice
scenarios.setting: You ask about a mild symptom and suitable OTC options.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Over-the-counter advice. You ask about a mild symptom and suitable OTC options. I'll start as the pharmacist; you reply as the customer. — I have allergy symptoms — what do you recommend that will not make me drowsy for work?

scenarios.title: Medication interaction check
scenarios.setting: You take a regular prescription and need OTC guidance.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Medication interaction check. You take a regular prescription and need OTC guidance. I'll start as the pharmacist; you reply as the customer. — I am on blood pressure medication — is this cough syrup safe with it, and what side effects should I watch for?

scenarios.title: Complex OTC triage
scenarios.setting: Multiple products failed; you seek a structured recommendation.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Complex OTC triage. Multiple products failed; you seek a structured recommendation. I'll start as the pharmacist; you reply as the customer. — I tried two antihistamines without relief — given my history, what active ingredient should we try next, and when should I see a clinician instead?

scenarios.title: Guardian picking up prescription
scenarios.setting: Consent and privacy intersect; you clarify responsibilities.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Guardian picking up prescription. Consent and privacy intersect; you clarify responsibilities. I'll start as the pharmacist; you reply as the customer. — I am picking up for my parent — what verification you need, what counseling you can share with me versus them, and how we handle dosage changes they may not remember?

scenarios.title: Cross-border prescription confusion
scenarios.setting: Regulatory framing matters; you avoid unsafe assumptions.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Cross-border prescription confusion. Regulatory framing matters; you avoid unsafe assumptions. I'll start as the pharmacist; you reply as the customer. — This was prescribed abroad — what equivalencies you can honor, what documentation closes liability, and what clinical red lines mean you must refuse despite urgency?

scenarios.title: Which bus
scenarios.setting: You are at a bus stop in a new city.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Which bus. You are at a bus stop in a new city. I'll start as the local passenger; you reply as the visitor. — Excuse me. I go to the museum. Is this the right bus?

scenarios.title: Transit directions
scenarios.setting: You confirm route and stop with a stranger.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Transit directions. You confirm route and stop with a stranger. I'll start as the local passenger; you reply as the visitor. — Does the 12 stop here for Central Library, and do I need a transfer?

scenarios.title: Night service and safety
scenarios.setting: You plan a return trip after an event.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Night service and safety. You plan a return trip after an event. I'll start as the local passenger; you reply as the visitor. — I need to get back after 11 — which routes still run, how frequent are they, and is there a safer stop if this one feels isolated?

scenarios.title: Disruption day travel
scenarios.setting: A strike or detour changes plans; you adapt.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Disruption day travel. A strike or detour changes plans; you adapt. I'll start as the local passenger; you reply as the visitor. — Official apps conflict — what is actually running on this corridor, what workaround locals use, and how much buffer I should add?

scenarios.title: Accessibility needs on transit
scenarios.setting: Mobility and anxiety interact; you ask for dignity-preserving options.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Accessibility needs on transit. Mobility and anxiety interact; you ask for dignity-preserving options. I'll start as the local passenger; you reply as the visitor. — I need step-free access and predictable crowding — which lines and times fit, what staff assistance exists, and how do I avoid being stranded if elevators fail?

scenarios.title: City mobility equity debate
scenarios.setting: A community meeting discusses service cuts; you argue a case.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: City mobility equity debate. A community meeting discusses service cuts; you argue a case. I'll start as the local passenger; you reply as the visitor. — Frame who bears the cost of this cut, what second-order effects on night-shift workers you are ignoring, and what revenue or priority shift would preserve coverage without magical thinking?

scenarios.title: Loud neighbor
scenarios.setting: Your neighbor is loud at night. You knock and speak simply.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Loud neighbor. Your neighbor is loud at night. You knock and speak simply. I'll start as the neighbor; you reply as the resident. — Hi. Sorry — the music is very loud. Can you make it quieter, please?

scenarios.title: Apartment noise
scenarios.setting: You ask a neighbor to lower volume after hours.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Apartment noise. You ask a neighbor to lower volume after hours. I'll start as the neighbor; you reply as the resident. — Hey — I have an early morning tomorrow; could you turn the bass down after 10?

scenarios.title: Noise pattern conversation
scenarios.setting: Repeated issues; you seek a practical agreement.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Noise pattern conversation. Repeated issues; you seek a practical agreement. I'll start as the neighbor; you reply as the resident. — This has been a few Tuesdays in a row — can we agree on quiet hours or moving speakers off the shared wall?

scenarios.title: Noise and lease norms
scenarios.setting: Relationship is strained; you reference building rules lightly.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Noise and lease norms. Relationship is strained; you reference building rules lightly. I'll start as the neighbor; you reply as the resident. — I want to avoid involving management — what schedule works for your gatherings, and can we text if it spikes so I do not have to knock cold?

scenarios.title: Cultural difference on noise
scenarios.setting: Interpretations of courtesy differ; you bridge without accusing.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Cultural difference on noise. Interpretations of courtesy differ; you bridge without accusing. I'll start as the neighbor; you reply as the resident. — Help me understand your norms versus mine — what you consider reasonable hours, what you did not realize carried, and what small change would feel respectful on both sides?

scenarios.title: Mediation-ready neighbor dispute
scenarios.setting: HOA may intervene; you propose a durable pact.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Mediation-ready neighbor dispute. HOA may intervene; you propose a durable pact. I'll start as the neighbor; you reply as the resident. — We are one complaint away from escalation — what measurable standard we both accept, what exceptions for holidays, and what third-party process you prefer if we slip?

scenarios.title: Gym — freeze
scenarios.setting: You will travel. You ask about your gym.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Gym — freeze. You will travel. You ask about your gym. I'll start as the gym front desk; you reply as the member. — Hello. I travel next month. Can I stop my gym for one month?

scenarios.title: Membership pause
scenarios.setting: You inquire about freezing membership for travel.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Membership pause. You inquire about freezing membership for travel. I'll start as the gym front desk; you reply as the member. — I will be away six weeks — do you offer a freeze, and what is the fee or notice period?

scenarios.title: Contract terms for injury
scenarios.setting: You hurt your knee; you need billing relief.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Contract terms for injury. You hurt your knee; you need billing relief. I'll start as the gym front desk; you reply as the member. — My doctor advised no squats for two months — what medical documentation you need to pause billing, and how reinstatement works?

scenarios.title: Membership dispute after move
scenarios.setting: Location closed; you negotiate transfer or exit.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Membership dispute after move. Location closed; you negotiate transfer or exit. I'll start as the gym front desk; you reply as the member. — My home club shut — what nearest option honors my rate, what commute is reasonable under your policy, and if neither works what exit path exists?

scenarios.title: Predatory clause challenge
scenarios.setting: Auto-renew surprised you; you seek principled resolution.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Predatory clause challenge. Auto-renew surprised you; you seek principled resolution. I'll start as the gym front desk; you reply as the member. — What notice you claim you sent versus what I received, what good-faith interpretation regulators expect here, and what resolution avoids small-claims but respects my time?

scenarios.title: Class-action adjacent pressure
scenarios.setting: Public thread names the chain; manager has latitude.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Class-action adjacent pressure. Public thread names the chain; manager has latitude. I'll start as the gym front desk; you reply as the member. — What systemic practice you will admit, what remediation batch you are authorized to offer, and what governance change you will document so this is not another PR cycle?

scenarios.title: Broken heat
scenarios.setting: Your apartment is cold. You call the landlord.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Broken heat. Your apartment is cold. You call the landlord. I'll start as the landlord; you reply as the tenant. — Hello. My heat is broken. It is very cold. Please help.

scenarios.title: Repair request
scenarios.setting: You report a maintenance issue in your rental.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Repair request. You report a maintenance issue in your rental. I'll start as the landlord; you reply as the tenant. — The bathroom fan stopped working and moisture is building — can you send someone this week?

scenarios.title: Habitability timeline
scenarios.setting: Heat fails in winter; you need a clear fix window.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Habitability timeline. Heat fails in winter; you need a clear fix window. I'll start as the landlord; you reply as the tenant. — It has been three nights without reliable heat — what is your plan, who is the contractor, and what temporary remedy can you authorize tonight?

scenarios.title: Repair vs rent withholding
scenarios.setting: Law is unclear locally; you negotiate without threats.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Repair vs rent withholding. Law is unclear locally; you negotiate without threats. I'll start as the landlord; you reply as the tenant. — I need this documented — what entry notice you will give, what standard you consider complete, and how we handle rent if the window slips again?

scenarios.title: Retaliation-sensitive tenancy
scenarios.setting: You fear pushback for complaining; you seek professional tone.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Retaliation-sensitive tenancy. You fear pushback for complaining; you seek professional tone. I'll start as the landlord; you reply as the tenant. — I want a paper trail that helps both of us — what timeline you commit to, what communication channel you prefer, and how we de-escalate if neighbors are also affected?

scenarios.title: Portfolio landlord leverage
scenarios.setting: Scale asymmetry; you argue for systemic accountability.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Portfolio landlord leverage. Scale asymmetry; you argue for systemic accountability. I'll start as the landlord; you reply as the tenant. — This pattern matches other units — what root cause in your vendor chain, what audit you will run, and what concession makes me confident this is not perpetual churn?

scenarios.title: Doctor office
scenarios.setting: A nurse asks why you came to the clinic.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Doctor office. A nurse asks why you came to the clinic. I'll start as the clinic nurse; you reply as the patient. — Hello. Why are you here today? Where does it hurt?

scenarios.title: Triage questions
scenarios.setting: You describe basic symptoms at a walk-in clinic.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Triage questions. You describe basic symptoms at a walk-in clinic. I'll start as the clinic nurse; you reply as the patient. — What is bothering you most today, how long has it lasted, and any fever?

scenarios.title: Structured symptom history
scenarios.setting: You prepare for a clinician with clearer chronology.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Structured symptom history. You prepare for a clinician with clearer chronology. I'll start as the clinic nurse; you reply as the patient. — Walk me through onset, what makes it better or worse, medications you took, and anything similar before?

scenarios.title: Chronic condition flare
scenarios.setting: You differentiate new red flags from baseline.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Chronic condition flare. You differentiate new red flags from baseline. I'll start as the clinic nurse; you reply as the patient. — I have a known condition — what changed this episode versus my usual flare, and what tests I should insist on versus wait out?

scenarios.title: Advocacy under time pressure
scenarios.setting: You feel dismissed; you stay evidence-based.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Advocacy under time pressure. You feel dismissed; you stay evidence-based. I'll start as the clinic nurse; you reply as the patient. — What differential you are considering, what data would change your plan, and how do I request a second opinion without poisoning this relationship?

scenarios.title: Cross-disciplinary care coordination
scenarios.setting: Specialists disagree; you force a synthesis.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Cross-disciplinary care coordination. Specialists disagree; you force a synthesis. I'll start as the clinic nurse; you reply as the patient. — What conflict exists between recommendations, what risk each stance underestimates, and what decision framework gets me to one accountable path this week?

scenarios.title: Food allergy
scenarios.setting: You order food and you have an allergy.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Food allergy. You order food and you have an allergy. I'll start as the server; you reply as the diner. — Hi. I cannot eat nuts. Is this dish safe for me?

scenarios.title: Allergy check at restaurant
scenarios.setting: You ask about ingredients before ordering.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Allergy check at restaurant. You ask about ingredients before ordering. I'll start as the server; you reply as the diner. — I have a shellfish allergy — does the kitchen share fryers or sauces I should worry about?

scenarios.title: Serious allergy order
scenarios.setting: You need staff to confirm with kitchen, not guess.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Serious allergy order. You need staff to confirm with kitchen, not guess. I'll start as the server; you reply as the diner. — I carry epinephrine — please confirm with the chef whether this dish contains dairy or traces, and what alternative you recommend if not.

scenarios.title: Cross-contamination risk
scenarios.setting: Menu is ambiguous; you push for clarity without drama.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Cross-contamination risk. Menu is ambiguous; you push for clarity without drama. I'll start as the server; you reply as the diner. — I need a conservative read — what prep surfaces touch nuts, what the kitchen can realistically segregate tonight, and what off-menu simple plate is safest?

scenarios.title: Restaurant liability conversation
scenarios.setting: A prior incident makes you vigilant; you stay collaborative.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Restaurant liability conversation. A prior incident makes you vigilant; you stay collaborative. I'll start as the server; you reply as the diner. — What protocol you follow for anaphylaxis risk, who signs off on exceptions, and how you document what you told me if something goes wrong?

scenarios.title: Group dinner with divergent dietary ethics
scenarios.setting: Host pressure meets medical need; you navigate gracefully.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Group dinner with divergent dietary ethics. Host pressure meets medical need; you navigate gracefully. I'll start as the server; you reply as the diner. — What compromise preserves my safety without making the table about me, what the kitchen can batch separately, and how do we reset if someone pushes “just a bite”?

scenarios.title: Lost wallet
scenarios.setting: You think you lost your wallet in a cafe.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Lost wallet. You think you lost your wallet in a cafe. I'll start as the cafe manager; you reply as the customer. — Hello. I lost my wallet. Did you find a black wallet?

scenarios.title: Lost item inquiry
scenarios.setting: You ask staff if anything was turned in.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Lost item inquiry. You ask staff if anything was turned in. I'll start as the cafe manager; you reply as the customer. — I think I left a brown wallet near the window — did anyone hand one in this morning?

scenarios.title: Lost property process
scenarios.setting: You need timing and verification steps.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Lost property process. You need timing and verification steps. I'll start as the cafe manager; you reply as the customer. — What is your lost-and-found policy, whether cameras can confirm timing, and what ID you need before you release an item to me?

scenarios.title: High-value loss emotions
scenarios.setting: You are stressed; you cooperate with process.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: High-value loss emotions. You are stressed; you cooperate with process. I'll start as the cafe manager; you reply as the customer. — Cards are frozen — help me reconstruct when I paid, whether staff saw it after, and what police report you recommend if it was stolen not lost?

scenarios.title: Good-faith dispute over found item
scenarios.setting: Another customer claims the same wallet; you navigate.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Good-faith dispute over found item. Another customer claims the same wallet; you navigate. I'll start as the cafe manager; you reply as the customer. — What evidence each party has, what neutral verification you will use, and how you avoid publicly shaming someone while resolving this?

scenarios.title: Reputation risk for small business
scenarios.setting: Social post alleges theft by staff; owner engages.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Reputation risk for small business. Social post alleges theft by staff; owner engages. I'll start as the cafe manager; you reply as the customer. — What footage policy you can share legally, what statement protects your staff without sounding defensive, and what restorative step rebuilds trust with regulars?

scenarios.title: School meeting
scenarios.setting: You meet your child’s teacher.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: School meeting. You meet your child’s teacher. I'll start as the teacher; you reply as the parent. — Hello. How is my child in class? Are they happy?

scenarios.title: Parent-teacher chat
scenarios.setting: You ask about behavior and reading at school.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Parent-teacher chat. You ask about behavior and reading at school. I'll start as the teacher; you reply as the parent. — How is she participating in group work, and what should we practice at home?

scenarios.title: Parent night follow-up
scenarios.setting: You discuss a mild learning concern constructively.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Parent night follow-up. You discuss a mild learning concern constructively. I'll start as the teacher; you reply as the parent. — I noticed math confidence dropped — what you see in class, what supports exist, and how we align without over-scheduling her?

scenarios.title: IEP-adjacent conversation
scenarios.setting: Formal plan not yet in place; you probe options.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: IEP-adjacent conversation. Formal plan not yet in place; you probe options. I'll start as the teacher; you reply as the parent. — What accommodations are informal versus documented, what timeline for evaluation if needed, and how do we track progress with shared metrics?

scenarios.title: Cultural mismatch with school norms
scenarios.setting: Discipline philosophy differs; you seek partnership.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Cultural mismatch with school norms. Discipline philosophy differs; you seek partnership. I'll start as the teacher; you reply as the parent. — Where your expectations clash with our home norms, what outcome you are optimizing for, and what language in emails reduces friction for my child in the middle?

scenarios.title: Gifted-and-struggling paradox
scenarios.setting: Complex profile; you push for nuanced plan.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Gifted-and-struggling paradox. Complex profile; you push for nuanced plan. I'll start as the teacher; you reply as the parent. — What strengths mask deficits, what boredom triggers behavior, and what acceleration versus scaffolding trade-off you will defend with administration?

scenarios.title: Card blocked
scenarios.setting: Your bank card does not work abroad.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Card blocked. Your bank card does not work abroad. I'll start as the bank phone agent; you reply as the customer. — Hello. My card does not work. I am in another country. Help.

scenarios.title: Travel card block
scenarios.setting: Fraud lock triggered; you verify identity.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Travel card block. Fraud lock triggered; you verify identity. I'll start as the bank phone agent; you reply as the customer. — I am traveling and my card declined — can you verify my account and lift the hold?

scenarios.title: Fraud alert triage abroad
scenarios.setting: You need cash access urgently.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Fraud alert triage abroad. You need cash access urgently. I'll start as the bank phone agent; you reply as the customer. — What transactions triggered the block, what security questions you need, and can you raise my ATM limit temporarily once cleared?

scenarios.title: Multi-factor failure on the road
scenarios.setting: SIM and app access are messy; you find a path.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Multi-factor failure on the road. SIM and app access are messy; you find a path. I'll start as the bank phone agent; you reply as the customer. — I cannot receive SMS — what alternate verification exists, what branch protocol applies internationally, and what provisional access you can grant?

scenarios.title: Regulatory hold nuance
scenarios.setting: Block is compliance-related; answers are limited.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Regulatory hold nuance. Block is compliance-related; answers are limited. I'll start as the bank phone agent; you reply as the customer. — What you can disclose versus cannot, what timeline resolution typically takes, and what documentation I should carry to satisfy the next review?

scenarios.title: Political risk banking
scenarios.setting: Sanctions or jurisdiction questions arise; you stay calm.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Political risk banking. Sanctions or jurisdiction questions arise; you stay calm. I'll start as the bank phone agent; you reply as the customer. — What rule triggered automated review, what innocent activity pattern mimics risk, and what escalation path exists without me stranded for weeks?

scenarios.title: Haircut words
scenarios.setting: You want a simple haircut.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Haircut words. You want a simple haircut. I'll start as the hair stylist; you reply as the client. — Hi. Please cut my hair shorter. Not too short. Thank you.

scenarios.title: Salon instructions
scenarios.setting: You describe length and style in basic terms.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Salon instructions. You describe length and style in basic terms. I'll start as the hair stylist; you reply as the client. — I want trim about two centimeters off, keep the layers, and tidy the neckline.

scenarios.title: Specific style reference
scenarios.setting: You bring a photo and negotiate feasibility.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Specific style reference. You bring a photo and negotiate feasibility. I'll start as the hair stylist; you reply as the client. — This photo is the vibe — my hair is finer; what is realistic, what maintenance it needs, and what if I do not style it daily?

scenarios.title: Color history complexity
scenarios.setting: Past dye affects options; you seek honest advice.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Color history complexity. Past dye affects options; you seek honest advice. I'll start as the hair stylist; you reply as the client. — I box-dyed six months ago — what damage you see, what color correction timeline you recommend, and what price band am I in?

scenarios.title: Identity-sensitive appearance change
scenarios.setting: You discuss gender-affirming or cultural sensitivity.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Identity-sensitive appearance change. You discuss gender-affirming or cultural sensitivity. I'll start as the hair stylist; you reply as the client. — What language avoids assumptions, what privacy I need in a busy salon, and how do you handle a style that may read differently in my workplace?

scenarios.title: Public figure discretion
scenarios.setting: Recognition risk; you negotiate privacy and photos.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Public figure discretion. Recognition risk; you negotiate privacy and photos. I'll start as the hair stylist; you reply as the client. — What policy on social posts, how we handle walk-ins recognizing me, and what fee structure reflects after-hours privacy?

scenarios.title: Return food
scenarios.setting: You want to return something you bought.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Return food. You want to return something you bought. I'll start as the store clerk; you reply as the shopper. — Hello. I want to return this. It is bad. Here is my receipt.

scenarios.title: Grocery return
scenarios.setting: A product is spoiled; you ask for exchange.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Grocery return. A product is spoiled; you ask for exchange. I'll start as the store clerk; you reply as the shopper. — This milk smells off before the date — can I swap it or get a refund with this receipt?

scenarios.title: Return policy edge case
scenarios.setting: Packaging opened; you plead reasonableness.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Return policy edge case. Packaging opened; you plead reasonableness. I'll start as the store clerk; you reply as the shopper. — I opened it before noticing mold — what proof you need, whether store credit is available, and how you handle allergens if kids ate some?

scenarios.title: Batch quality issue
scenarios.setting: You suspect wider problem; you escalate calmly.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Batch quality issue. You suspect wider problem; you escalate calmly. I'll start as the store clerk; you reply as the shopper. — Three items from the same brand failed — should this be logged centrally, what batch codes help, and what goodwill gesture reflects repeat customer status?

scenarios.title: Food safety advocacy
scenarios.setting: You want accountability without vilifying frontline staff.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Food safety advocacy. You want accountability without vilifying frontline staff. I'll start as the store clerk; you reply as the shopper. — What upstream check failed, what you will pull from shelves tonight, and how you communicate to customers who already purchased?

scenarios.title: Supplier chain accountability
scenarios.setting: Regional manager is present; you argue systemic fix.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Supplier chain accountability. Regional manager is present; you argue systemic fix. I'll start as the store clerk; you reply as the shopper. — What root cause analysis you will run, what transparency to regulators if needed, and what compensation framework avoids case-by-case lottery?

scenarios.title: Park path
scenarios.setting: You are in a park and need simple directions.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Park path. You are in a park and need simple directions. I'll start as the park ranger; you reply as the visitor. — Hello. Where is the lake? Is it far to walk?

scenarios.title: Trail information
scenarios.setting: You ask about difficulty and time for a hike.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Trail information. You ask about difficulty and time for a hike. I'll start as the park ranger; you reply as the visitor. — Is the blue loop okay for beginners, and how long does it usually take?

scenarios.title: Weather and safety
scenarios.setting: Clouds roll in; you adjust plans.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Weather and safety. Clouds roll in; you adjust plans. I'll start as the park ranger; you reply as the visitor. — If lightning risk rises, what shelters exist, what turnaround point you recommend, and how fast conditions change at elevation here?

scenarios.title: Wildlife encounter protocol
scenarios.setting: You saw signs of bears; you want guidance.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Wildlife encounter protocol. You saw signs of bears; you want guidance. I'll start as the park ranger; you reply as the visitor. — What recent sightings, what behavior actually reduces risk versus myth, and should I reroute with kids?

scenarios.title: Conservation tension with recreation
scenarios.setting: Crowds damage habitat; you engage ethically.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Conservation tension with recreation. Crowds damage habitat; you engage ethically. I'll start as the park ranger; you reply as the visitor. — What carrying capacity data you track, what visitor behavior you need to change, and how enforcement pairs with education so it does not feel punitive?

scenarios.title: Climate-stressed ecosystem
scenarios.setting: Fire season policy shifts; you seek nuanced guidance.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Climate-stressed ecosystem. Fire season policy shifts; you seek nuanced guidance. I'll start as the park ranger; you reply as the visitor. — What long-term change you are managing, what trade-off between access and protection you are making, and how should advocates help without political theater?

scenarios.title: Dentist time
scenarios.setting: You need another day for the dentist.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Dentist time. You need another day for the dentist. I'll start as the dental receptionist; you reply as the patient. — Hello. I cannot come Tuesday. Can I come Friday?

scenarios.title: Reschedule appointment
scenarios.setting: You move a dental cleaning.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Reschedule appointment. You move a dental cleaning. I'll start as the dental receptionist; you reply as the patient. — I need to shift next week’s cleaning — what openings do you have after 4 p.m.?

scenarios.title: Procedure prep reschedule
scenarios.setting: Imaging timing affects treatment plan.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Procedure prep reschedule. Imaging timing affects treatment plan. I'll start as the dental receptionist; you reply as the patient. — If I move the prep appointment, does that push the procedure date, and what fasting rules still apply?

scenarios.title: Insurance window pressure
scenarios.setting: Benefits reset soon; you optimize slots.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Insurance window pressure. Benefits reset soon; you optimize slots. I'll start as the dental receptionist; you reply as the patient. — What latest date still clears insurance this cycle, what codes you will bill, and what out-of-pocket if we slip to January?

scenarios.title: Dental anxiety negotiation
scenarios.setting: You need sedation options discussed plainly.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Dental anxiety negotiation. You need sedation options discussed plainly. I'll start as the dental receptionist; you reply as the patient. — What sedation tiers you offer, what risk profile I fit, and what consent language I should understand before agreeing?

scenarios.title: Overtreatment skepticism
scenarios.setting: Second opinion culture; you probe recommendations.
scenarios.firstLine: Scene: Overtreatment skepticism. Second opinion culture; you probe recommendations.
